title: The AMS overlay already canonized the subscriber-not-wire architecture — gap inventory found four genuine gaps not three
content: D0010 (irreversible) plus observability-payload-boundary (Tier 1) plus observability-as-subscriber (Tier 2 principle) cover the architectural decision, the safety boundary, the hook+subscriber split, and the capabilities declaration. The remaining gaps were: (1) state-totals/cardinality strategy distinct from activity-stream sums; (2) wire-layer latency vocabulary distinguishing TTFF from LLM TTFT; (3) token-count derivation procedure on subscribers (cl100k_base); (4) HMAC promotion to AMS reference deployment default. Items 1-3 were unaddressed; item 4 was permitted-as-stronger-option but not chosen as default.

title: Provider usage blocks cannot enter the AMS wire — earlier planning answer was wrong for this service
content: An earlier planning turn proposed using provider `usage` blocks (Anthropic, OpenAI) as the billing-canonical token source with cl100k_base as fallback. AMS is a broker between agents (AMS.md §3); agents call providers directly; provider responses including usage metadata never traverse the wire. The wire-layer token count is necessarily an approximation; the correction is recorded in canon/principles/token-count-derivation-on-subscribers.md §"What the Subscriber Cannot Compute".

title: TTFT applied to wire-layer measurements would be a category error — coined TTFF instead
content: A `token` frame in AMS may carry one LLM token, a chunk, a complete utterance, or non-LLM bytes (PROTOCOL.md §7 keeps data opaque). Calling first-frame latency "TTFT" imports a meaning the wire cannot guarantee. The new principle (canon/principles/wire-layer-latency-vocabulary.md) coins TTFF (time to first frame) as the wire-layer metric, restricts TTFT to anchored conversations (voluntary `model_invoked` metadata), and forbids silent substitution.

title: D0013 — state totals via per-minute Cron snapshot worker, reusing existing 6-double slots
content: Per-minute Cron worker reads operational stores (KV for accounts/conversations, DO enumeration for live streams), writes one row per metric per tick to the same `ams_telemetry` Analytics Engine dataset as the activity stream. event_type='state_snapshot', metric name in endpoint_or_close_code (blob 2), value in peer_count_at_event (double 5) — slot reuse rather than schema expansion. Door type two-way (removing the worker leaves wire conformance intact). Cost: ~5,800 writes/day, well under AE free tier. Authoritative at ams://canon/decisions/D0013-state-totals-via-snapshot-worker.

title: HMAC-SHA-256 promoted to AMS reference deployment default for identifier hashing
content: Boundary doc amended in §"Hashing and Pseudonymization" — HMAC-SHA-256 with sink-local secret (provisioned as `AMS_TELEMETRY_HASH_KEY` Cloudflare Workers secret), truncated to 12 hex chars, becomes the AMS reference default. Unkeyed SHA-256 retained as documented downgrade. Threat model: read access to telemetry sink + candidate account_id should not be sufficient to confirm a match. Rotation = one-way break with prior aggregations (also a right-to-be-forgotten purge mechanism).

title: Slot reuse over slot expansion when extending an active hook-layer schema
content: Both new doc additions (D0013 snapshot worker + token-count derivation aggregations) ride peer_count_at_event (double 5) with event_type discriminating semantics, rather than expanding the 6-double allocation. Reasoning: every new slot ripples through every existing dashboard query and every existing analytics consumer. Slot reuse with discriminating event_type keeps the schema flat, the SQL one-table, and the upgrade path zero. The pattern is named informally — could be promoted to its own canon principle if the next overlay extension faces the same call.

title: The subscriber-as-telemetry-collector pattern is "simple vodka" — telemetry never enters the hot path
content: Operator framing during planning: "It's just a subscriber. Not in the middle to slow it down." This is exactly D0010's polymorphic-subscriber model applied to observability. The token-count-derivation principle codifies the rule: tokenization happens off the broker's hot path on the subscriber's runtime. The broker emits to every subscriber by its broadcast rule; the observability subscriber tokenizes the bytes in its own runtime. No hot-path coupling.

title: Existing AMS canon house style uses frontmatter (tier/stability/governs) for confidence signals — explicit "Confidence:" headings would bloat
content: oddkit_challenge's prerequisite checklist asked for confidence/disconfirmer/reversibility signals on each of the 4 new artifacts. A general-purpose checklist; AMS canon already encodes these via frontmatter `tier`, `stability`, `status`, `governs` plus body sections "What Forecloses" and "What This Is Not". The pattern is implicit. D0013 added an explicit "Door Type, Confidence, and Retraction" section because the gauntlet's reversibility pressure on Tier-2 decisions deserves the explicit door classification per SPEC §9 vocabulary. Two principle docs left implicit per existing house style.

title: oddkit_audit scopes to the canonical knowledge base writings/ tree by default — overlay canon needs manual link integrity check
content: `oddkit audit canon` against knowledge_base_url=https://github.com/klappy/agent-messaging-service scanned the upstream `writings/` tree, not the AMS overlay's `canon/` directory. Returned 0 findings. Manual link integrity audit on new artifacts found one real issue: `klappy://docs/oddkit/tools/telemetry_public` does not resolve; canonical URI is `oddkit://tools/telemetry_public`. Fixed in docs/observability-dashboard.md §"See Also". All other ams:// and klappy:// URIs in new artifacts resolved.

title: Day 3 PR #21 is on close codes 4001/4002/4005/4500 + examples/two-agents/ — zero overlap with observability gap-fill
content: Verified before writing: PR #21 modifies worker/src/{conversation,index}.ts and adds examples/two-agents/ + a poc-build-repeatability principle. Does not touch canon/decisions/, canon/constraints/observability-payload-boundary.md, canon/principles/observability-*, or any telemetry surface. D0013 numbering is also unused on the PR. The amended boundary doc is the only file in this gap-fill that overlaps a file Day 3 might also touch — and Day 3 doesn't touch it. Safe to land in parallel.

title: Five artifacts produced for operator review; spec-for-next-version drafting remains
content: Files added: canon/decisions/D0013-state-totals-via-snapshot-worker.md (Tier 2, 191 lines after door-type addition), canon/principles/wire-layer-latency-vocabulary.md (Tier 2, 156 lines), canon/principles/token-count-derivation-on-subscribers.md (Tier 2, 167 lines), docs/observability-dashboard.md (service docs, 277 lines). Files amended: canon/constraints/observability-payload-boundary.md §"Hashing and Pseudonymization" + frontmatter (amendments field added; complements expanded). Total: ~960 new lines + ~10 amended. Per the user's sequencing ("docs first, spec for next version, then build"), next session drafts the v1.next observability spec referencing these canon URIs. Build follows spec acceptance.

title: Long-form spec docs not modified in this pass per AMS canon-additions discipline
content: Following the discipline established in 2026-05-01-ams-observability-canon.tsv H row: canon overlay extracts load-bearing constraints without rewriting long-form docs. SPEC.md §5 deferred-items entry "DOLCHE journal observability subscriber" (line 108) and §12 horizon entry "Observability subscriber" (line 216) remain unchanged. ARCHITECTURE.md §8 staging unchanged. The new canon URIs are now addressable references those long-form sections should point at when the operator opens them; that update is not in this pass.

title: v1.2 spec authored as a separate draft file (SPEC-v1.2-OBSERVABILITY-DRAFT.md) rather than in-place edit to SPEC.md
content: Two reasons drove the file split. First, Day 3 PR #21 is in review against main on related code paths (worker/src/{conversation,index}.ts) and editing SPEC.md in parallel would have created either merge conflict or false drift; the canon files added in this gap-fill don't conflict with PR #21 but SPEC.md is the lock and editing it without an explicit accept gate would have collapsed the operator's "docs first → spec → build" sequencing. Second, the operating contract pattern (canon/principles/contract-governs-handoff-drift) treats spec edits as commitments; a draft file held in /SPEC-v1.2-OBSERVABILITY-DRAFT.md keeps the lock unchanged while the operator reviews. On accept, §14 "Diff Against v1.1.1" of the draft enumerates exactly the SPEC.md edits needed; the draft moves to journal/ at that time.

title: Forward-compatibility check against HORIZON.md per SPEC §14 — pass
content: HORIZON.md grep for telemetry/observability/metric/audit/cost confirmed v1.2 observability is the first shipping instance of HORIZON §4 (Governance and Oversight): fact-checker subscriber (§4.1), cost monitor (§4.3), audit logger (§4.5) are all explicitly anticipated as polymorphic-subscriber patterns. v1.2 enables that whole class by establishing the polymorphic-observability-subscriber pattern in production. Forecloses no catalog entry. The forward-compat check is recorded in the spec draft §14 "Forward-compat check (per SPEC §14)".

title: The spec's smoke test §3.1 needs 7 items (vs v1's 5) because observability has more wire-invisible verification surfaces
content: v1 PoC §3.1 had 5 smoke items because the wire was the entire surface — connect, send, receive, MCP wrap. v1.2 adds three layers (hook, snapshot, subscriber) plus two metric vocabularies (TTFF/TTFT, token-count) plus secret provisioning. Each acceptance criterion needed an explicit falsifiable verification because the dashboard reading the wrong number from a missing layer would still show a number — failure is silent unless the smoke test asks for a specific row to exist. The 7 items map 1:1 to the v1.2 layers: secret bound, hook-layer write, hook-layer write on failure, snapshot worker writes 4 metrics, observability subscriber connects + emits, TTFF measured + labeled, token-count derivation produces non-zero. Future minor-bumps that add layers should follow the same pattern: one smoke item per layer of new infrastructure, written as a falsifiable SQL or curl assertion.

title: Spec draft staged at SPEC-v1.2-OBSERVABILITY-DRAFT.md awaiting operator review
content: On accept, the §14 diff drives the SPEC.md update: title to v1.2; §4 Scope IN gets 7 new items; §5 Scope OUT loses the "DOLCHE journal observability subscriber" row (re-entry signal fired) and gains the v1.2-deferred items; §6 architecture diagram updated; §7-12 add v1.2-specific rows; §13 references add the new canon URIs and dashboard doc; §14 revisions table gets the new row drafted in the file. Pre-build checklist at the end of the spec drives the next phase: HMAC secret provision, AE binding, 8 hook call sites, snapshot Cron, reference observability subscriber Worker, capabilities convention amendment, smoke + DoD evidence, deeper docs alignment per release-validation-gate.

title: D0014 supersedes D0013 — Tail Worker for hook layer + activity-stream-derived state totals; no scheduled jobs anywhere
content: Operator pre-execution challenge during spec-draft review surfaced two distinct brittleness sources in D0013's design: (1) inline writeDataPoint() in the AMS Worker required every new handler to remember the call (forgotten = silent invisibility); (2) per-minute Cron snapshot worker depended on best-effort Cron Trigger reliability (missed runs = silent gaps in headline numbers). Verified current CF service shapes (Tail Workers + AE pattern is CF's documented recommendation; Pipelines is open beta but sinks to R2/Iceberg, not AE). Authored ams://canon/decisions/D0014-tail-worker-and-activity-stream-derived-totals which: (a) replaces inline hooks with a Tail Worker bound via tail_consumers — AMS Worker becomes 100% telemetry-free, removability proof becomes trivial; (b) replaces Cron snapshot worker with activity-stream-derived totals via AE's uniqExact() and SUM() — zero polling, single SQL surface, recovery automatic when activity resumes. Fidelity gap on do_evicted explicitly named and accepted for v1.2 with three remediation options enumerated for v1.next. D0013 marked superseded with header note + frontmatter status update; documents value as record of rejected per-request-hook alternative.

title: The spec was the right place to surface this — pre-execution challenge before code lands is much cheaper than post-build redesign
content: The user's challenge ("could all of this be simplified using other CF services?") arrived after the spec was drafted but before code started. Mode contract handled this correctly: reverted to planning, surfaced the alternatives with honest tradeoffs, asked one load-bearing question (DO health fidelity), then re-executed on accept. The cost was ~30 minutes of revision; the saved cost is ~2-3 days of building the wrong thing followed by either tolerating brittleness or refactoring under production pressure. The pattern: when a draft spec presents a fragile architecture, hold execution and re-challenge. The contract anticipates this — gates between modes exist precisely to make this cheap.

title: Tail Worker + activity-stream-derived totals is more vodka than the inline-hook + Cron-snapshot pattern by every test
content: Vodka-architecture four-question test (per ams://canon/principles/vodka-architecture-applied) applied to D0014 vs D0013: (1) Has the broker grown thicker? D0013 added 8 inline call sites; D0014 removes them entirely (broker thins). (2) Has it acquired domain opinions? Neither does, but D0014's removal of telemetry from the hot path is more honest. (3) Can it be removed without consequence? D0013 removability requires source edits; D0014 removability is a single config-line unbind (tail_consumers). (4) Is it maintainable by one person indefinitely? D0013 has two scheduled-job classes to monitor (Cron reliability, hook coverage); D0014 has zero scheduled jobs and uniform observability across all events. D0014 wins on every axis except do_evicted fidelity, which is a v1.next decision.

title: The fidelity gap on do_evicted is the only honest cost of the simpler design — explicitly named, not papered over
content: D0014 §"The Honest Fidelity Gap (DO Eviction)" names the cost without minimizing it: silently-evicted DOs cause streams_live to overcount until clients reconnect. Three remediation options enumerated as v1.next (hourly probe, DO-alarm heartbeats, orphan-correction worker). Operator chose option 1 (drop snapshot entirely) over option 2 (hourly probe) — preferring maximum vodka over partial fidelity protection. Re-entry signals named in the canon. Dashboard footer discloses the gap so operators don't misread streams_live as exact.

title: Spec revision complete: D0013 superseded, D0014 added, SPEC-v1.2 draft updated, dashboard SQL recipes updated, journal updated
content: Files in this revision: D0013-state-totals-via-snapshot-worker.md (frontmatter + body header updated to mark superseded); D0014-tail-worker-and-activity-stream-derived-totals.md (new, ~200 lines, Tier 2 decision); SPEC-v1.2-OBSERVABILITY-DRAFT.md (substantive edits to §§1-2, §3.1 smoke test, §3.2 demo gate, §3.3 DoD, §4 Scope IN, §5 Scope OUT, §6 Architecture diagram, §7 Alternatives, §8 Risks, §9 Reversibility, §10 Disconfirmers, §11 Open Decisions, §12 Horizon, §13 References, §14 Diff + revisions row + pre-build checklist); docs/observability-dashboard.md (Headline Numbers section, State-Total Trends section, Activity-stream liveness, Honesty Footers, See Also). All ams:// URIs verified to resolve against the current overlay; D0014 added to the file inventory.
